title-group.article-title: Intermittent fasting and a no-sugar diet for Long COVID symptoms: a randomized crossover trial

contrib-group.aff: 2https://ror.org/009c06z12grid.414785.b0000 0004 0609 0182Intermountain Medical Center Heart Institute, Salt Lake City, UT USA
3https://ror.org/00f54p054grid.168010.e0000 0004 1936 8956Division of Cardiovascular Medicine, Department of Medicine, Stanford University, Stanford, CA USA
4Arkansas Colleges of Health Education/Arkansas College of Osteopathic Medicine, Fort Smith, AR USA
5https://ror.org/008d6qw11grid.449174.b0000 0004 0398 9002Pacific Northwest University of Health Sciences, Yakima, WA USA
6https://ror.org/00thqtb16grid.266813.80000 0001 0666 4105University of Nebraska Medical Center, Omaha, NE USA
7https://ror.org/00cvxb145grid.34477.330000 0001 2298 6657University of Washington, UW Medicine, Seattle, WA USA
8Community Memorial Health System, Ventura, CA USA
9https://ror.org/00h6set76grid.53857.3c0000 0001 2185 8768Statistics Department, Utah State University, Logan, UT USA

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

abstract.p: Long COVID (LC) is a common chronic health condition that impairs daily functioning and social connections. This is the first randomized clinical trial to directly compare the effect of two Intermittent Fasting regimens on LC symptoms. The main objectives of this 10-week randomized cross-over trial are to compare the efficacy and safety of 4 weeks of 1–2 day fasting plus a restricted diet vs 4 weeks of mild time-restricted eating (TRE) and a restricted diet in reducing patient-reported LC symptoms. After a 2-week run-in, subjects were randomized to treatment A (TRE) or treatment B (Fasting) for 4 weeks. Subjects then crossed over to the other treatment for 4 weeks. The median fasting duration was 38 h (night-day-night), and the mean duration was 42 h. Symptoms were assessed via weekly online surveys. Primary outcomes were changes in LC symptom severity scores (LC-Scores) and in the number of LC symptoms (numLCsym) between treatments. Secondary outcomes were changes in LC-Scores and numLCsym over the 10-week trial. Fasting was superior to TRE alone in reducing LC-Scores (p = 0.008). The numLCsym decreased − 5.0 during the Fasting 4 weeks vs − 1.4 in the TRE 4 weeks (p = 0.002). Altogether, the 10-week regimen of a no-sugar diet, TRE and Fasting decreased the mean LC-Score by 51.8% (p < 0.0001) from 37.8 to 18.2. Similarly, numLCsym decreased from 20.5 to 12.2, a decrease of 40.6% (p < 0.0001). No major adverse safety events were recorded. Both intermittent fasting interventions decreased symptoms over the 10-week trial but the more intense fasting regimen was significantly better.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Long COVID (LC) is a major global public health problem1. It is an often-debilitating post-acute COVID-19 condition with a wide range of symptoms that overlap with other chronic health conditions such as ME/CFS, multiple sclerosis, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, and chronic Lyme disease2–5. Fatigue, post-exertional malaise (PEM), and ‘brain fog’ are three of the most prevalent symptoms6. While the underlying pathophysiology of LC is unknown, likely hypotheses include viral persistence, persistence of viral remnants, unusual T cell activation, autoimmunity and chronic inflammation2,7–9. Although a number of emerging LC treatments are under investigation such as hyperbaric oxygen therapy10, epipharyngeal abrasive therapy11, amantadine12 and stellate ganglion blocks13, currently no clinically proven treatments or validated diagnostic tests exist14–16. Several early clinical trials attempting to treat Long Covid symptoms have yielded negative results and generally many potential pharmaceutical interventions are not yet in clinical trials17–19.

Fasting is the voluntary, deliberate cessation of nutrient intake for an extended period, usually for more than 14 consecutive hours. Intermittent fasting is repeated episodes of fasting conducted for a duration of up to 2 consecutive days at a frequency of once or twice per week, or every other day (alternate-day fasting), or fasting for a daily duration of 14–18 h as time-restricted eating (TRE) with a daily eating window of 6–10 consecutive hours20. An observational study found that hospitalization and death in the acute and post-acute phases of COVID-19 were lower in people engaging in periodic fasting21, and two case reports have indicated that fasting may reduce LC symptoms22,23.

The lower energy and nutrient status during fasting could potentially be beneficial in several ways. Glucose metabolism, lipid metabolism, mitochondrial health, oxidative status and autophagy are all influenced via key cellular sensors such as AMPK, SIRT1 and mTOR24–26. Specifically, induction of autophagy has been demonstrated to occur during fasting in animal models due to inhibition of mTORC127,28. The autophagy pathway can degrade dysfunctional mitochondria via mitophagy, clear protein aggregates and other damaged cellular components, decrease inflammation, and enhance cellular energy management29,30. Autophagy also stimulates antiviral innate immunity31,32 while SARS-CoV-2 inhibits both the autophagy and IFN signaling pathways33–37. Fasting is known to activate autophagy, enhance antigen presentation, assist viral clearance, decrease autoimmunity by enhancing Treg cells and decrease inflammation38–41. While fasting and autophagy are well studied in animals, little is known about the fasting duration needed to stimulate macroautophagy in humans42,43. In healthy young males, a 17–19 h fast increased two autophagy transcripts, however this was a dry fast that included no water43. Another study that examined shorter intermittent fasting found increased expression the autophagy gene LC3A with a 6 h early eating window compared to a 12 h eating window44. In brief, while it is clear that fasting and ketogenic diets affect autophagy in people, there is little data on the optimal fasting and diet regimen to realize the positive effects of autophagy. It is presumed that longer fasting durations of > 20 h that increase human growth hormone and ketone levels also stimulate autophagy. In short, Fasting may counteract multiple pathways adversely affected by SARS-CoV-2 infection and stimulate antiviral immune responses45. This trial tested whether a no-added sugar diet and a more intense intermittent fasting regimen of 1–2 days of consecutive fasting per week coupled with a 16:8 TRE regimen (i.e., 16 h of fasting and an 8-h eating window each day) reduced LC symptoms more than a mild 14:10 TRE regimen.

sec.title: Study design and treatments
sec.p: This patient-led research study is based on the experience of Dr. Bunker with his 5+ year long-haul and his interactions with others from the LC community. This randomized crossover clinical trial (NCT06214455) (19/01/2024) was approved by the Pacific Northwestern University of Health Sciences Institutional Review Board and was registered at clinicaltrials.gov prior to initiation of screening and enrollment. One exclusion criterion was added per a study amendment in May of 2023 to exclude those with a pre-existing chronic health condition such as ME/CFS, EDS or chronic Lymes disease. The study followed the Consolidated Standards of Reporting Trials (CONSORT) guidelines for randomized clinical trials, with the exception that study enrollment was stopped early due to challenges involving the conduct of a trial by an all-volunteer team and slower than anticipated subject enrollment. All research was performed in accordance with relevant human subject guidelines/regulations including the Declaration of Helsinki. All enrollees provided informed consent via electronic signature within a study REDCap instrument and were sent a copy of their signed informed consent46,47.

The two period, two treatment (2 × 2) crossover design was chosen to compare two intermittent fasting treatments. Treatment A (hereafter called TRE) was a no-sugar, optionally no wheat diet with a 10–12 h daily eating window and water-only fasting for the other 12–14 consecutive hours. Treatment A is similar to other TRE regimens considered to be intermittent fasting20,48. Treatment B (hereafter called Fasting) was the same no-sugar diet with a more restrictive 8-h daily TRE eating window and 16 consecutive hours of daily fasting, along with a once weekly 23–60 h water-only fast. The length of the 23–60 h fast was selected by the participant, with the goal of a 36-h night-day-night fast or a 60-h fast (3 nights, 2 days). The actual length of each fast was recorded each fasting week by participants. Because symptoms were assessed 5 days after the fasting period, no “wash-out” period was incorporated between the two treatment periods. Due to the nature of the interventions, the trial was not blinded. A 2-week “run-in” period prior to the first intervention period was practiced by all participants using the TRE regimen (i.e., no-sugar diet, daily 10–12 h eating window). Supplements that induce autophagy were excluded and the 2-week run-in allowed a baseline to be established.

This trial assessed 60 patient-reported symptoms and broadly defined LC. The weekly LC Symptom Severity surveys asked subjects to also report any flare-ups and adverse events they experienced within the last 7 days. Because of the anticipated high frequency of LC flare-ups, flare-ups that were “routine” from the participant perspective were considered minor adverse events. Flare-ups that included symptoms that were not routine were considered expected adverse events. To minimize participant cognitive fatigue, the weekly survey averaged 8 min completion time.

sec.title: Trial participants
sec.p: Key inclusion criteria were ages from 18 to 69 years old; having 5 or more of 28 common LC symptoms, residing in the United States, and willingness to halt supplements linked to autophagy induction. Exclusion criteria included BMI < 20, pregnancy or breastfeeding, SARS-CoV-2 infection within the last 45 days, diabetes, and history of an eating disorder. Full eligibility criteria are available in the trial protocol.

Between November 2022 and April 2024, a total of 424 individuals were prescreened via pragmatic electronic means. Of these, 105 proceeded to screening and enrollment (Fig. 1) via video interview and consenting. A total of 96 participants enrolled in the study, however, 19 (20%) of these did not actually begin the study baseline. The “attempted treatment” sample consisted of 77 participants, of whom 58 participants (75%) successfully completed the 10-week study as designed and, thus, comprised the analytic sample. Dr. Bunker enrolled all participants and randomly assigned them to crossover trial treatment arms based on whether their REDCap identifier was even or odd. Hence, the randomization did not stratify on the number of symptoms, symptom severity, illness duration, biological sex, or other factors.Fig. 1
CONSORT study flow diagram (2 × 2 crossover design).

sec.title: Primary outcomes
sec.p: The two primary outcomes were the change in LC-Score and the change in numLCsym during the 4 weeks of TRE vs the 4 weeks of Fasting. LC-Score was calculated from weekly surveys using a 0–4 severity scale for each of 28 patient-reported symptoms commonly found in LC. For LC-Score, symptom severity values for the 28 common symptoms were summed for each participant and then added to the number of 32 additional less-common symptoms reported as present or not, regardless of severity. The numLCsym was a count of all 60 symptoms present, in which the 28 common symptoms were considered absent if severity was 0 and present if severity was 1–4.

sec.title: Secondary outcomes
sec.p: Secondary outcomes were changes in LC-Score and numLCsym during the 10 weeks of the study, including the 2-week baseline, 4 weeks of TRE, and 4 weeks of Fasting. Exploratory outcomes included measure changes during the 2-week baseline and changes in 28 symptom frequencies over 10 weeks. Subgroup analyses and evaluation of individual symptoms were also completed.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Participant characteristics were summarized as counts with percentages overall and in each treatment arm (AB and BA) or by means with standard deviations (SD) in those groups. Presence of individual symptoms at baseline, end of the run-in period, end of Fasting, end of TRE, and end of the 10-week trial were summarized using percentages or means.

To examine global changes in all participants for LC-Score and numLCsym, Student’s t-test was used to test differences between means at baseline and the end of the 2-week run-in phase, as well as baseline and the end of the 10-week trial. Further, a repeated measures general linear model was used to examine changes across all 11 measurements of LC-score and numLCsym from baseline to the end of week 10. Subgroup evaluations used the unpaired t-test or before-after analyses with Student’s t-test, as appropriate, to examine changes of LC-Score and numLCsym for Fasting versus TRE in strata defined by quartiles of overall change to determine effects of the interventions for high and low responders. Other stratified analyses were examined based on age, sex, timing of first appearance of LC (post-infection or post-vaccination), and other baseline characteristics. Subgroups with non-normal data sets were analyzed using Mann–Whitney tests. Safety of the treatments were evaluated for expected LC flare-ups and for major adverse events.

Analyses were two-sided using GraphPad Prism and p ≤ 0.05 was designated as statistically significant. Treatment differences were analyzed using paired, 2-tailed t tests for normally distributed data, Wilcoxon rank sum tests were used for non-normally distributed data.

While two primary outcomes were defined (LC-score and numLCsym), these were highly correlated measures of the same hypothesis with correlations of changes in LC-score and numLCsym of r = 0.883 across the 4 weeks of Fasting and r = 0.843 across the 4 weeks of TRE. Due to that high concordance, no correction of the p-value was made for the primary outcome analyses. As no prior study involving fasting and LC symptoms existed, sample size was estimated based on 2022 understanding and publications about LC, and the expected effect of fasting on LC symptoms. The trial Statistical Analysis Plan is included in the Trial Protocol. Per reviewer request, the primary crossover trial outcomes were also determined via two-way ANOVA, to account for mixed effects and to better assess carryover effects.

sec.title: Participant characteristics and restricted diet adherence
sec.p:
Overall, 58 participants (75%) completed the 10-week study protocol as designed. The mean (SD) age is 49.5 (11.1) years and 42 (72.4%) are female. Other baseline characteristics are presented in Table 1. Like other LC studies, females have worse LC based on both mean LC-Score (40.5 vs males: 30.8) and numLCsym (21.6 vs males: 17.6). Cumulatively, 91% have received at least one COVID-19 vaccination and 78% have received a booster vaccination. 18 (31.0%) participants reported that their symptoms first began soon after receiving a COVID-19 vaccination. The mean (SD) duration of participant symptoms entering the study is 24.6 (10.7) months. The self-reported weekly adherence rate for the no-added sugar diet is 95.7%. The adherence rate during the fasting 4 weeks’ 8–9 h eating window is 94.0%. For the TRE 4 weeks’ 10–12 h window adherence rate is 84.1%. The mean Fasting eating window is 8.3 h and the mean TRE window is 10.1 h. In the analytic sample, 56 of the participants completed all four fasts and 2 completed three fasts.Table 1
Baseline characteristics of study participants.
(a) CharacteristicAll subjectsFemalesMales
Group participants, number584216
Age, median (SD)49.5 (11.1)49.3 (9.4)49.9 (14.5)

Initial numLCSym (SD)20.5 (8.2)21.6 (8.7)17.6 (7.1)
Initial LC-Score (SD)37.8 (19.9)40.5 (20.1)30.8 (17.8)

sec.title: Decrease in LC-Score and numLCsym
sec.p:
The mean decrease in LC-Score (Fig. 2a, Supplementary Table S1) during the Fasting 4 weeks was − 10.2 ± 13.6 compared to − 2.8 ± 11.6 in the TRE four weeks (p = 0.008). The numLCsym (Fig. 2b) was also reduced significantly, with changes of − 5.0 ± 5.6 due to Fasting compared to − 1.4 ± 4.8 during TRE weeks (p = 0.002).Fig. 2
(a) Violin plot of the change in LC-Score during the 4 weeks of each study treatment. The mean values are shown in bold font next to the bold dashed line. The 25th and 75th percentiles that bound the interquartile ranges are shown as dotted lines. Individual data points are shown as diamonds. A Wilcoxon matched pairs test of the two treatment groups is statistically significant with p = 0.008. The median values are − 1.0 and − 8.0 respectively for the TRE and Fasting groups (not shown). (b) Violin plot of the change in numLCsym during the 4 weeks of each studytreatment. The mean values are shown in bold font next to the bold dashed line. The 25th and 75th percentiles that bound the interquartile range are shown as dotted lines. A two-tailed paired ttest of the two treatment groups is statistically significant with p = 0.002. The median values are − 0.5 and − 5.0 respectively for the TRE and Fasting groups (not shown).

The best responders were scattered throughout the severity spectrum of initial LC-Score (Fig. 3a). The two study arms showed a divergence in their mean LC-Score during the first four treatment weeks and a convergence during the last 4 weeks, reflecting greater improvements from Fasting (Fig. 3b). Some modest carryover from the fasting weeks in the “Fasting then TRE” arm may have been present, but this was not statistically significant (p = 0.08). The lack of statistically significant carryover was confirmed via 2-Way ANOVA analysis where the variation due to sequence x treatment (p = 0.268) and due to sequence or order of treatments (p = 0.303) were both non-significant. The 10-week treatments decreased mean LC-Score for all participants from 37.8 at week-0 to 18.2 at week-10 (− 51.8%) (p < 0.0001) (Fig. 4a).Fig. 3
(a) Long Covid Symptom Severity Scores by participant: week 0 and week 10. Participants (n = 58) are sorted by decreasing initial Week 0 LC-Score (black bars) with their end score (blue bars) adjacent to their initial score. At the end of their 10-week study participation, 3 individuals have higher scores, 1 was unchanged and 54 (93%) have lower scores. Note the overall linear initial baseline severity distribution rather than a normal or bell curve distribution, reflecting a highly variable number of patient-reported symptoms at baseline (ranging from 38 to 5). Five or more common symptoms were needed for study eligibility. (b) Weekly mean LC-Score by trial arm. Each data point are means of 29 participant Long Covid Symptom Severity Scores by trail arm at each weekly timepoint with 95% confidence intervals. Week 0 is baseline, weeks b1 and b2 are the end of the baseline run-in weeks, and weeks 1–4 and 5–8 are the two 4-week treatment periods of Fasting or Time-Restricted Eating. Trial phases are separated by a vertical dashed line. The initial and ending difference between the trial arm group LC-Score means are 3.9 and 6.3. (c) Changes in mean LC-Score during trial phases, stratified by quartile. All participants were ranked by percent decrease in LC-Score from best responders to worst responders. Q1 is the top quartile of responders over the 10-week trial duration including the 2-week baseline run-in, and completion of either the Fasting then TRE arm or the TRE then Fasting arm. The mean change in LC-Score during each of the given trial phases is plotted here. Note, for half of participants, the order in which they did the treatments was different (TRE then Fasting) then that chosen for the plot.Fig. 4
Violin plots of Week 0 vs. week 10 measure distributions. (a) LC-score: Long COVID Symptom Severity Scores (LC-Score) for 58 trial participants are shown as violin plots. This figure compares the Week 0 time point distribution to the Week 10 time point distribution. Per a Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed rank test the P value is < 0.0001. The mean LC-Score for all participants (bold dashed line) decreased from 37.8 at baseline to 18.2 at the end of the 10-week trial. The 25th and 75th percentile in each group are shown by dotted lines. (b) numLCsym: Number of Long Covid Symptoms (numLCsym) for 58 trial participants are shown as violin plots. This plot compares their Week 0 time point distribution to their Week 10 time point distribution. Per a Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed rank test the P value is < 0.0001. The mean numLCsym for all participants (bold dashed line) decreased from 20.5 at baseline to 12.2 at the end of the 10-week trial. The 25th and 75th percentile in each group are shown by dotted lines.

Based on overall 10-week change in LC-Score, the top quartile of “best responders” has a mean 10-week decrease of over − 90% while the bottom quartile has a mean decrease of -5.9% (Fig. 3c). Unexpectedly, the best responder quartile, Q1, has a mean decrease of − 16.4 (− 42.9%) in baseline 2-week LC-Scores while Q4 has no decrease. The baseline run-in 2 weeks and the 4 weeks of TRE decreased LC-Score in the two best responding quartiles but not in the two worst responding two quartiles. See Tables 2, 3 for numeric LC-Score and numLCsym results in quartiles of overall response.Table 2
Quartiles sorted by overall 10-week change in Long Covid Symptom Severity Scores (LC-Score).
10-week change in mean LC-Score measure with participants grouped into quartiles based on overall percent decrease in the measure. Q1 is the group of best responders while Q4 is the group of worst responders during the 10-week trial. The mean change in the measure is shown for each phase of the trial; 2-week Baseline time-restricted eating, 4-week Fasting, and 4-week time-restricted eating. Note, for half of participants, the order in which they did the Fasting and TRE treatment phases was opposite the order that was chosen for the table.Table 3
Quartiles sorted by overall 10-week change in number of Long Covid symptoms (numLCsym).
10-week change in mean numLCsym measure with participants grouped into quartiles based on overall percent decrease in the measure. Q1 is the group of best responders while Q4 is the group of worst responders during the 10-week trial. The mean change in the measure is shown for each phase of the trial; 2-week Baseline time-restricted eating, 4-week Fasting, and 4-week time-restricted eating. Note, for half of participants, the order in which they did the Fasting and TRE treatment phases was opposite the order that was chosen for the table.

The 10-weeks of intervention decreased mean numLCsym from 20.5 to 12.2 (− 40.6%) (p < 0.0001) (Fig. 4b). The top quartile of “best responders” has a mean decrease of − 86.3% while the bottom quartile has a mean increase of 3.8% (Table 3). Using a − 30% threshold, 76% have a decrease in their LC-Scores and 66% have a decrease in the number of their numLCsym. Interestingly, better 4-week Fasting responses were not correlated with subject total fasting duration (i.e., 23–60 h) (Supplementary Fig. S1). When primary analyses were adjusted for four severe flare-ups at endpoints, the p-values for the Fasting vs TRE treatment decreased slightly.

sec.title: Symptom severities
sec.p: Like the decrease in LC-Scores and numLCsym, mean symptom severities for the 28 tracked common symptoms also decreased. For illustration, Supplementary Fig. S2 shows a heatmap of the mean symptom severity of each symptom during the 10-weeks of the trial for the top-half of good responders as measured by the percent change in LC-Score. Overall, the 28 common Long COVID symptoms saw a decrease in mean severity of − 80.6% in this subgroup of good responders. Tinnitus and ‘Nerve Pain’ have the smallest average decrease in severity of − 53.1% and − 64.0%. ‘Light sensitivity’ and headache have the largest average decrease in mean severity of − 96.4% and − 93.1%. For comparison, the overall decrease in LC-Score for the top-half of responders was − 80.0% and − 70.8% for the overall decrease in numLCsym.

sec.title: Symptom frequencies and responsiveness
sec.p:
Fatigue (98.3%), Post-exertional malaise or PEM (84.5%), Difficulty Concentrating (86.2%), and Brain Fog (82.8%) are the four most frequent baseline symptoms. Initially, 22 of 28 common symptoms were present in 50% or more of the participants (Fig. 5a). A general pattern was observed in good responders where the severity of symptoms decreased first, and then some symptoms would go away. No participants had all mild symptoms at the start of the trial but 10 weeks later, on the final survey, 15 participants have either no symptoms (n = 2) or only mild symptoms (n = 13). The overall 28 symptom frequency decreased from a mean of 59.5% to a mean of 34.5% at the end of the trial (Fig. 5b).Fig. 5
(a) Overall Long COVID symptom frequencies: initial and end. The Initial (week 0) and End (week 10)frequencies of the 28 common patient-reported LC symptoms for which the trial tracked the severity on a 0–4 scale (0 = none, 1 = mild, 2 = moderate, 3 = serious, 4 = severe). (b) 10-week change in LC symptom frequencies. Violin plot of the 28 common LC symptom frequencies at the Week 0 and Week 10 time points. At week 0, the mean frequency for all 28 symptoms was 59.5% and 10 weeks later the mean frequency for the 28 symptoms was 34.5%. Per a Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed rank test, this is statistically significant with a P value of P < 0.0001. The initial week 0 and ending week 10 frequencies for individual symptoms are shown in (a); Week 0 symptoms are “Initial” and Week 10 symptoms are “End”.

Resolution of individual symptoms during the 10-week trial varied considerably (Fig. 6). Eleven of 28 common symptoms resolved about 51% of the time (46–56%) while another eleven symptoms resolved about 40% of the time (35–44%). Meanwhile, blurry vision, tinnitus, fatigue, and PEM resolved in only about 20–24% of participants. Frequencies of the less-common 32 symptoms tracked also decreased 40% overall (Supplementary Table S2).Fig. 6
Percent resolution by LC symptom. Participant-reported symptoms varied in their responsiveness to treatment during the 10-week trial. The percentage of participants who reported one of 28 common Long COVID symptoms on their initial symptom survey but did not report it on their final symptom survey after 10 weeks is the percent resolution. Brown color is for constitutive symptoms (4), aqua is for neurological symptoms (8), purple is for psychosocial symptoms (3), light green is for neurocognitive symptoms (4), forest green is for neuromuscular symptoms (4), pink is for GI symptoms (1) and blue is for cardiopulmonary symptoms (4). Categories are per a network analysis clustering of patient-reported symptoms64.

sec.title: Flare-ups
sec.p:
LC flare-ups typically last for a day or two, but rarely a week or more6,49. Overall, 52 of 58 subjects (90%) reported at least one symptom flare-up during the 10-week study. The mean number of flare-ups was 4.4 (0,10). The two most common attributions for flare-ups were overexertion (28.1%) and water fasting (27.3%) (Fig. 7). During the Fasting 4-weeks, 58% of flare-ups were attributed to the fasting treatment. Of the total flare-ups, 18.6% were during the baseline 2-weeks, 47.0% were during the Fasting 4-weeks and 34.4% were during the TRE 4-weeks.Fig. 7
Flare-up attributions.52 of 58 participants (90%) reported at least one symptom flare-up during the 10-week trial. The mean number of flare-ups reported was 4.4 [0,10] for a total of 253 flare-ups. 32 of 58 participants (58%) reported one or more fasting related flare-up.

sec.title: Adverse events and weight loss
sec.p: Two severe AEs (hospitalizations) were reported. One was for acute appendicitis before the participant attempted Fasting. The other was overnight hospitalization for left-side pleurisy, inflammation, and bicep tendonitis after 38-h of fasting (Supplementary Table S3). A total of 47 adverse events were reported by 26 participants during the trial. Of these, 8 were deemed ‘probably related’ to Fasting and 6 were deemed ‘possibly related’. Altogether, 14 of 47 (29.7%) adverse events were probably or possibly related to Fasting. In general, adverse events were tolerable and no participants elected to stop their study participation or were disenrolled due to them. The mean decrease in BMI over 10 weeks was − 1.2 kg/m2 (4.6%) from the initial mean of 25.9 kg/m2. Subjects with an initial BMI between 20 and 25 kg/m2 saw a mean decrease of − 0.7 kg/m2. Four subjects ended with BMIs of 18.5–20 kg/m2. In terms of weight, participants on average lost − 2.5 lbs., − 1.3 lbs., and − 3.7 lbs. during the baseline 2 weeks, the TRE 4 weeks, and the Fasting 4 weeks respectively.

sec.title: Subgroup analyses
sec.p: Age-stratified analyses showed the younger group (age < 47.5 years) has a mean 10-week LC-Score change of − 70.2% while the older group has a mean change of − 44.3% (p = 0.004). Better 10-week responses also correlated with several baseline characteristics including greater daily steps (p = 0.008), symptoms first starting within 2 weeks of COVID-19 vaccination (p = 0.019), symptoms beginning with the Omicron wave or later (p = 0.029), fewer non-fasting flare-ups (p = 0.030), and a daily activity level greater than ‘just chores’ (p = 0.058). While most individual baseline symptoms did not correlate with overall responses, the absence of ‘leg weakness’ (p = 0.025) and moderate, mild or no insomnia at baseline did correlate with better 10-week responses (p = 0.010). Better 10-week responses were not associated with sex, baseline BMI, initial LC-Score, initial numLCsym, or duration of LC (Supplementary Table S4). Subgroup analyses stratified by weight loss were not significantly associated with decreases in LC-Score during the trial phases, although weight loss greater than 2.5 lbs. nearly reached statistical significance during the baseline 2 weeks (p = 0.054). Two baseline diet characteristics, ‘no wheat’ and dairy consumption, also did not reach statistical significance. While the numbers are too small for statistical significance, a single ‘half-pack-a-day’ smoker was an excellent 10-week responder, and four daily alcohol drinkers were poor responders. Better Fasting 4-week responses were correlated with greater baseline daily steps (p = 0.003) and symptoms first starting post-vaccination (p = 0.026).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The findings of this clinical trial demonstrate that a more-intense Fasting regimen of daily 16:8 TRE and once-per-week ≈1.5-day water-only fasting combined with a no-added-sugar diet reduces LC symptoms more than a less-intense 14:10 TRE diet. It also shows that both the more intense Fasting intervention and the less-intense TRE intervention were effective in reducing LC symptoms across the full 10-weeks of the clinical trial. To our knowledge, this is the first randomized trial to directly address whether intermittent fasting reduces or resolves symptoms of LC and the first to compare a mild and more intense fasting protocol.

Subgroup analyses were able to identify several baseline characteristics correlated with better responses in the trial. Younger age may be a positive factor because of stronger immune responses and fewer senescent cells. Those that averaged > 4500 steps per day in the baseline 2 weeks could have a lower viral load and/or less mitochondrial dysfunction. Finally, having serious or severe insomnia correlated with poorer responses. Sleep is known to be important for maintaining circadian rhythms, healthy immune function, and brain neuroplasticity50,51.

One assumption with the trial’s crossover design is that Long COVID is a chronic health condition. Indeed, the mean duration of Long COVID symptoms for subjects in this trial was over two years. A recent longitudinal cohort study reported less than 2% recovery in a cohort where the mean duration was 2 years52. Another study reported 46% and 57% recovery from fatigue and cognitive deficits respectively between 9 and 26 months53. In that study the overall trend was gradual improvement, but it optimistically considered mild fatigue or mild cognitive deficits as “recovered”. In the current trial, participant mean LC-Scores were stable after the baseline 2 weeks with time-restricted eating as shown in Fig. 3c for the TRE then Fasting group. Despite frequent individual flare-ups, the mean LC-Score stability supports the validity of the chronic illness assumption.

Many factors influence energy balance including metabolic rate, calorie intake and calorie expenditures. A detailed analysis is not possible as we did not measure daily caloric intake. However, the study did track daily steps, fast duration, and weekly weight. In a subgroup analysis we noted a significant correlation between baseline daily steps walked (> 4500) and better responses. Yet, longer fasting durations did not correlate with greater Long COVID improvements. Furthermore, while there may be a weak correlation between greater weight loss during the fasting 4 weeks and symptom improvement measures, this did not reach statistical significance. A few of the top quartile responders did not lose any body weight and some of the bottom quartile responders lost 10 pounds or more. Hence, a negative energy balance does not appear to be the main factor responsible for symptom improvement.

While the mechanisms by which the wide variety of LC symptoms are ameliorated are unknown, the induction of autophagy is likely a significant factor because it can decrease inflammation and increase viral clearance27,38,41. Nutrient depletion during short-term fasting likely induces transient macroautophagy, degrading a percentage of viral proteins in infected cells, enhancing antigen presentation and stimulating antiviral immune responses39. Further, the detection of viral RNA by pathogen recognition receptors stimulates innate immunity, autophagy and localized inflammation via Type I Interferons39,54–56. Type I Interferon production signals neighboring cells and activates hundreds of Interferon Simulated Genes; many of which have antiviral effects against RNA viruses57. Autophagy is integral to the process of viral antigen presentation via MHC-II and promotes antigen presentation to T Helper cells that direct immune responses including activation of cytotoxic T cells and NK cells. Autophagy also enhances antigen presentation on MHC-Class 1 to cytotoxic T cells via cross-presentation58. Hence, it is likely that antiviral immune responses induced via fasting selectively target tissues and cells with SARS-CoV-2 viral persistence.

Many viruses, including SARS-CoV-2, may drive cells to senescence causing possible age-related tissue dysfunction59. Indeed, research has shown that SARS-CoV-2 activates the cGAS-STING pathway in endothelial cells and causes dysfunctional mitochondria; releasing mtDNA that also activates the cGAS-STING pathway60,61. The stimulation of mitophagy via Intermittent Fasting may reduce the pro-inflammatory senescent associated secretory phenotype (SASP) and is consistent with reduced muscle and joint pains, memory issues, and word difficulty.

Many participants experienced some fasting related symptom flare-ups, and this is consistent with possible stimulation of antiviral innate immunity. The linkage of innate immunity to LC symptoms is also suggested by the participants (31.0%) who reported that their symptoms began soon after a COVID-19 vaccination (Supplementary Table S5)62,63. Persistent SARS-CoV-2 may be unusually good at avoiding immune responses as it actively blocks the later stages of autophagy and Type I interferon signaling pathways33–37. We propose that intermittent fasting treatments stimulate host-defense mechanisms that may suddenly make a previously hidden SARS-CoV-2 infection visible to the immune system.

While the reduction of such a broad range of patient symptoms in such a relatively short timeframe does not distinguish between likely hypotheses as to the root cause, it does suggest they may all share a similar underlying pathobiology. Regardless of the mechanisms at play, this clinical trial demonstrates the potential of manipulating cellular metabolism via diet, TRE and periodic short duration fasts to significantly reduce LC symptoms.

sec.title: Strengths and limitations
sec.p: Strengths of the trial include the crossover design that compared two different fasting protocols in the same individuals. This alleviates the difficulties associated with enrolling a parallel and comparable control group for a previously untested hypothesis and increases the statistical power of the study. Another strength was the all-remote, low-burden, patient-centered pragmatic design with no need for participants to travel for clinical visits. Limitations include no follow-up symptom surveys after the intervention period to assess the durability of patient gains. This trial relied on patient-reported symptoms and severity assessment since there are no available biomarkers for Long COVID. Misspecification of symptom-driven outcomes may provide less precision in the assessment of actual LC endpoints. Finally, although subjects were excluded if they had a prior chronic health condition, changes in other factors not related to the fasting interventions may have impacted participant symptoms, which could bias outcomes.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: In this randomized crossover trial measuring 60 participant-reported Long COVID symptoms, 1–2 day weekly water fasts together with daily 16-h fasting significantly decreased both LC scores and number of LC symptoms compared to 14-h daily fasting alone. Both symptom severities and frequencies decreased over the full trial timespan. Wide variation was observed in how well individuals responded to the 10-week intervention. The top quartile of responders saw decreases in LC-scores of over 90% and 86% in the number of LC symptoms. While 55% of participants reported one or more transient treatment-related flare-up, 1–2 day fasts appear safe for most people with LC. Additional clinical trials are warranted to further characterize this potential treatment and could be combined with blood tests for assay of biomarkers and various immunological characteristics. Intermittent fasting might also be useful as a complementary treatment when pharmacological options emerge.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

The original online version of this Article was revised: In the original version of this Article Jeffrey Novack was incorrectly affiliated with ‘Arkansas Colleges of Health Education/Arkansas College of Osteopathic Medicine, Fort Smith, AR, USA.’ Their correct affiliation is ‘Pacific Northwest University of Health Sciences, Yakima, WA, USA’. The original Article has been corrected.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1038/s41598-025-07461-0.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: We gratefully acknowledge the conscientious, dedicated long-haulers who supported this study with their active participation. We appreciate the assistance of PNWU IRB administrators; Carla Case and Jeremy Simmons. Thanks to Heather Brummer for reviewing nutrition guidelines in the participant instructions. Special thanks to Lucy Horne for additional statistical analysis and review following the sudden death of her father Benjamin D Horne.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: Dr. Bunker had full access to all of the data in the study and takes responsibility for the integrity of the data and the accuracy of the data analysis. Concept and design: Bunker, Novack Acquisition, analysis, or interpretation of data: Bunker, B Horne Drafting of the manuscript: Bunker, B Horne Statistical analysis: Sorrells, B Horne, L Horne Administrative, technical, or material support: L Horne, Gribble, Laube, Solomon, Baldwin Supervision: Novack Deceased: After the manuscript was submitted, B Horne died on 28/12/2024.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: Qualified researchers or other individuals may send data access requests to Thomas Bunker PhD., to obtain a copy of the de-identified n = 58 analytic dataset.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
